candidates.stmt:
A Camden resident since 1990, I have also been a Lib Dem member all that time, more active since 2014 when I retired from my work as an arts charity director. For the previous 10 years, I had been director of VocalEyes, which works to make the arts accessible to blind and partially-sighted people through words.

In 2015/16, I campaigned hard to Remain in the EU and am proud that Camden voted 75% to remain in the EU. My voluntary projects now include being a Schools ambassador for CAFOD, a trustee of Opera Up Close and also a trained tour guide for Camden; while training, my project walk was created around Gospel Oak. I also sing with Highgate Choral Society which regularly performs at All Hallows, Gospel Oak. feel honoured to be selected as a Lib Dem candidate for Gospel Oak and am eager to ensure the residents get a good deal from listening Lib Dem councillors.

Hi, my name’s Matthew Kirk and I am proud to be the Parliamentary Candidate for Holborn and St Pancras.

I have practised as a barrister specialising in criminal law for nearly twenty years and spend my working life fighting to secure justice and protect the rights of all concerned in the criminal justice system.

I live in Kentish Town with my family. North London is where my roots are, as I am reminded whenever I pass the Royal Free, where my brother was born and my grandfather died.

My family and I are building our lives in this community. My daughter is at a local primary school. My wife, Katya Apekisheva, is a part of our rich cultural scene in Camden as a regular performer at the wonderful Kings Place and artistic director of the London Piano Festival.

I believe we need to take urgent action to confront the existential threat of climate change and environmental collapse, and to rescue our public services. But first we must resolve the Brexit crisis.

I have campaigned to promote European cooperation for over twenty-five years. I am passionate in my belief that the country has made a terrible mistake and must have the chance to think again. For our future peace and prosperity, and to find solutions to the problems we face, we must Remain.

I’ve lived in our area for six years and feel exceptionally lucky to be part of our beautiful community and to bring up my young kids here. I’m standing to be your local councillor because this is my home.

I’ve always been committed to public service. As a young military officer, I was injured while serving in Iraq. Since then, I’ve been a blind person and I’ve worked in higher education, volunteered for Camden NHS Trust’s mental health services, and advised the government on issues related to disability and mental health.

I have a firm grasp of how local issues and council decisions impact some of our most vulnerable residents. If elected, I would work hard for you and be a sensible and compassionate voice on Council.

I first came to Belsize in the late 1980s, living on Belsize Lane, and quickly fell in love with the area. I’m passionate about protecting and enhancing this exceptional ward — its rich history, vibrant community, and unique character must be safeguarded while allowing it to thrive.

Today, my two grandchildren attend local schools, and I take great pleasure in enjoying the streatery, often sitting there after collecting them. It’s a small but special part of daily life that reflects the spirit of Belsize.

With a strong background in business and community service, I bring leadership, determination, and practical problem-solving skills to get things done. I understand the key local concerns — from supporting small businesses and responsible development to tackling crime and improving public services.

Belsize deserves a councillor who listens, takes action, and delivers. I’m ready to serve, and I hope to earn your support in the upcoming election.

I’m standing for the Green Party in Bloomsbury because I believe Camden Council must do more to ensure affordable, warm, safe housing for local renters, students and homeless residents alike. If elected, I promise to be a councillor who works hard for everyone in Bloomsbury and fights for a Camden everyone can afford to live and thrive in.

As a resident of Bloomsbury, I deeply care about the wellbeing of my local community. You deserve local representatives who are local residents and true public servants who put your needs above all else, not party careerists serving their own interests.

As a student standing in Bloomsbury, a ward that’s home to numerous universities, I am particularly motivated to represent students’ interests and perspectives, which are often underrepresented. Students experience housing and other local challenges in unique ways, and their problems have consistently been ignored by the council. I want to change this because I value students' experiences, and because I believe that a Council which is inclusive of everyone who lives and works in the local community would make Camden Council better at meeting _everyone's_ needs.

I am also committed to supporting and protecting local immigrant communities, especially at a time when attacks and hostility toward immigrants are on the rise. Ensuring that everyone in Camden feels safe, welcome, and heard is central to my desire to serve on the council. I am committed to advocating for full divestment of council funds from investments in fossil fuel companies, arms companies, and companies complicit in genocide in Gaza, focusing instead on investing in the local communities councillors are elected to serve and represent.

It would be an honour to be your councillor, and if elected, I would do everything in my power to ensure that Bloomsbury is a place you can afford to live and thrive in.

"I'm proud of Bloomsbury's radical history. Let's live up to that legacy: Decent homes. Care for the environment. Invest in the community instead of genocide, and stop corporations sucking money out of our neighbourhood."

I am standing as a candidate because I believe in treating people with fairness and kindness. Human Rights are a part of everyday life, and our current council has failed to protect key human rights that would enable residents to thrive, not just survive. Decades of labour has seen the council grow grossly complacent and blind to the suffering of residents, allowing our most vulnerable neighbours to slip through the cracks and into a life of unknowns. We need better housing, better support, better care for the elderly, those at risk, and the unhoused. We need smarter spending, and we need to listen to the people of Camden.

My Name is Richard Samuel. I have practiced as mainly a family law solicitor in Camden for over 30 years.

I am known to many of the local people for trying to deal with the many of the Housing problems that exist on my estate which is run down and poorly repaired if at all. Many flats are full of vermin. Nothing appears to get done but the council bills keep coming!

I became fed up with the ongoing and continuous council waste from endless leaflets informing me of the obvious " drink water in hot weather" and telling me to keep warm in the winter to most recently the road closures and re wilding schemes that are costing the taxpayer £millions. These leaflets cost £thousands that can be better spent.

The council needs to concentrate on their core duties of cleaning and repairing the streets and its properties, providing schooling and maintaining libraries etc not vanity projects wasting scarce money.
 
I decided to join Reform Uk as I have just had enough of government both local and national and wanted real change not "Starmer change" where things get worse, taxes raised and businesses fail.

I hope that some of you agree with me.

Vote Reform on May 7th!!

I moved to London and settled in a bedsit on Dennington Park Road in West Hampstead in 1974. Before moving to Fortune Green where I now live I lived briefly on Netherwood Street then on to Ariel Road. I lived on Aries Road when my daughter was born.

I have lived in West Hampstead and Fortune Green for all my adult life. I have experience and campaigned with others for the ThamesLink redevelopment. Like most people in the area, I had hoped for a more radical Station redevelopment to include all three stations and reduce the congestion we see every morning outside West Hampstead Underground station.

I was a founder member of the Fortune Green and West Hampstead Neighbourhood Development Forum which was formed to represent the views of residents in the decision-making process of local planning at Town Hall. I am extremely disappointed that local residents views on 136 West End Lane were ignored by Camden Planners. I shared the views of residents in Lymington Road whose skyline will be overshadowed by the proposed development.

I am part of St James Church and I was pleased that the local Post Office was relocated to this Community facility at the Sherriff Centre. I took part in lobbying for this to happen.

I am a nurse and have worked in the NHS for over 40 years.

Farrell is a strong community campaigner and will bring a fresh energy, a stronger voice to residents who have been overlooked by Camden Labour. As a committee member that organises the annual Jester Festival on Fortune Green, a member of Friends of Fortune Green and the West Hampstead Amenity and Transport Group, Farrell is working alongside Cllr Nancy Jirira to improve everyday life in Fortune Green.
He is passionate about social mobility, having spent his career working with charities to help young people reach their potential. He is a Trustee and Treasurer of the Magic Circle Foundation, as well as works on a cross-party basis to tackle the rise of far-right extremism and has served on the boards of several charities, giving him a strong track record of responsibility and ensuring organisations work for the people they serve, driven by a commitment to fairness.

I have lived in Fortune Green since 2011, and my children have grown up here. I am passionate about education, the environment, clean energy, safety, equality, social justice, fighting hate, and celebrating diversity. I serve on many council panels, including the Safer Neighbourhoods Panel, and I am active in the local community.

Camden is a huge borough, yet too often the council applies one-size-fits-all approaches that do not reflect local needs. Consultations can give a veneer of democracy while community voices are ignored, developers break environmental commitments, and urgently needed improvements are held up by red tape. I am determined to hold Camden Council to account, challenge harmful decisions, and ensure that funds and policies genuinely benefit residents.

Professionally, I have worked in telecoms since 1995, bringing broadband to rural communities and delivering innovative solutions. I specialise in tackling difficult problems others avoid and will bring that same determination to serving Camden.

My name is Steven Adams and I’ve lived in Hampstead all my life, as have my grandparents, parents, children. My children and I all attended school here. I wanted to make a difference in our community so I’ve been a governor of the major Frognal Independent School for the last 5 years.

For 35 years I have been a practicing architect with my firm based in Belsize Park. Combined with being Chair of a Conservation Area Advisory Committee, I will ensure that all planning and construction proposals are safe and well-communicated to our community. 

As a previous Councillor for Belsize Ward, I achieved many successes assisting residents. In 2022 I championed local initiatives such as the Belsize Streatery and helped residents resist misguided transport proposals made by Camden Council. I worked together with Council Housing groups to fight off unpopular planning applications and sat on the Licencing and Housing Scrutiny Committees to protect our environment. My position on the Council was always firm in support of residents. I am committed to driving progress, creating improvement, and understanding the background of all local issues. 

I fully understand the machinations of local authority planning, development, and transport design. Residents need their Councillors to be aware of city-wide and national policies that affect our community. Along with Cllr Andrew Parkinson we will defend Frognal against the distant and uncaring camden Town Hall.

While I am not religious, I am entirely supportive of other peoples’ beliefs and appreciate different cultures. My Conservative values align with aspiration, self-sufficiency, and supporting other people. This is why I have been connected with charities for most of my life and raised money cycling in over 25 countries around the world. 

My aim has always been to promote sensible Conservative values and show others that our Party is effective, kind, and competent in government. 

With all this in mind, I look forward to working to

Charles Harris is a best-selling author and writer-director for cinema and TV, who has won international prizes for his work, including films campaigning for the environment and fair trade in food. He joined the Green Party when it was still the Ecology Party. He has lived and worked in Frognal and Fitzjohns Ward since 1983, taking a particular interest in local affairs and the quality of life in the neighbourhood.

Willow has lived in Camden all her life and is passionate about giving back to the community she grew up in. She currently serves on the National Labour Students Committee and is Co-Chair of her university’s Labour Club. If elected, Willow would continue Camden Labour’s work of standing up for the most marginalised in our society, especially young people who are underrepresented in the political process.

Richard lives in Oak Village and is employed as a data engineer in the energy sector. He is a parent and environmentalist who sees the benefit of active travel such as walking, cycling and public transport. Richard is also a scout leader for 8th Holborn.

Raising children has given me 
opportunities to step outside my 
comfort zone. Managing a team and 
volunteering weekly at Cubs has 
taught me the patience and leadership 
needed to represent a diverse ward.

I know that Queen’s Crescent can feel 
ignored after repeated consultations. I 
hear local concerns around social 
housing, cleanliness, public toilets, and 
cycle parking.

Andrea moved back to Gospel Oak 3 years ago and works with children during the day. Through getting involved in her local tenants and residents association she could see how her estate has been neglected by the council and hopes to make improvements that local people need across all estates in the ward.

Rob is an English and maths tutor. He has been a volunteer for the homelessness charity Crisis for the past 5 years and is concerned about loneliness and social isolation which was the focus of his recent PhD studies.

I’m standing to represent Haverstock because residents deserve honest leadership and real results. I will fight for better housing repairs, tackling damp, delays, and poor standards. I will push for fair, transparent estate regeneration, where residents shape decisions. I will prioritise community safety, tackling antisocial behaviour and working for cleaner, safer streets. On LTNs, I support practical, balanced solutions that reduce pollution without ignoring local concerns.
My focus is simple: put residents first and hold the council accountable.

Alice lives with her family near Queen’s Crescent, and her husband Tom runs the bread stall on Queen’s Crescent market. She works as an architect in Archway, and her professional focus is retrofitting existing buildings and conservation.

Locally, she is involved with community groups including Better West Kentish Town, Climate Emergency Camden, and Retrofit Kentish Town. She is chair of the West Kentish Town Conservation Area committee and was an advisor to Kentish Town Neighbourhood Forum.

As your councillor, Alice will hold Camden to account on housing repairs and maintenance, and building additional social homes. She will campaign to protect the environment and for better planning for people and planet.

After moving to London for his studies, James quickly fell in love with the city. The stunning buildings, vibrant high streets, and incredible people of Camden inspired him to make this borough his home. Now, he’s committed to giving back to this borough, a place that has given him so much over the years.

Professionally, James is dedicated to helping businesses in their transition to net zero, and he works in energy and sustainability policy. If elected, he will fight for stronger protections for renters and leaseholders, enhance our beautiful green spaces and children’s play areas, and ensure our streets are safe for everyone,addressing both crime and air pollution.

I have lived in London for 28 years & for 25 of those years I haves lived in NW6. I am married to a Londoner & I have two children, one of whom attends a local primary school. I work as a Family Learning Teacher & a Parental Engagement Practitioner. Since 2008, I have been closely involved with the "Friends of Maygrove Peace Park" - a local community group within the Fortune Green ward. I would describe myself as a very ordinary working Mum but I am passionate about improving facilities & services in my local area.

Davi has lived in West Hampstead for 39 years. She is a council tenant and is particularly interested in housing issues and in adult education for those suffering from mental ill-health.

Davi is an active member of the local community. She is a supporter of the Kilburn Older Voices Exchange (KOVE) and developed a colouring workshop for the over 50s which KOVE supported.

In 2017 Davi won an award for services to the voluntary sector for her work at the Sidings Community Centre. She is a Volunteer Support Worker at Sidings where she helps the over 50s to learn and improve their computing skills. She is currently developing a workshop for the HUB in Swiss Cottage which is a Mental Health and Wellbeing Centre.

I am proud to call Camden my home, especially because I grew up facing limited opportunities. My parents have always worked incredibly hard as responsible citizens, even though their efforts often went unnoticed. Their dedication truly inspires me, and I am grateful for the community that surrounds us.

But over the years, I have seen the growing gap between us —the residents—and the Labour-led council in Camden. With rising crime, overdevelopment, unsafe neighbourhoods, and antisocial behaviour, there is a real sense of mistrust between us and the council. Is Camden listening? No!

These experiences have taught me that we must act to serve and protect our community, as true service comes from action, not just ambition.

This is why I am proud to be part of the Primrose Hill Community Association and The Friends of Regent’s Park and Primrose Hill, committed to supporting residents—especially the vulnerable—restoring residents’ voices, and revitalising our community. As a Local School Governor, I engage with parents, staff, and the wider community to ensure that our pupils achieve the best educational standards. By participating in the Primrose Hill Safer Neighbourhoods Team (PHSNT) panel meetings, I share consistent and timely information with the police, helping to foster a safer community and maintain a high quality of life for residents in Primrose Hill.

My focus is on ensuring we have clean and safer neighbourhoods. We need a visible police presence, more CCTV coverage, and increased street lighting to help us feel safe in Primrose Hill. As your prospective Councillor, my priority will be to fight for Primrose Hill to receive its fair share of community neighbourhood policing from the Metropolitan Police. I also aim to restore due diligence in development and consultation processes by promoting in-person consultations instead of relying on online robots, to help rebuild trust between residents and the council. Primrose Hill residents must be heard before final

Camden has been home to three generations of my family, spanning more than 50 years. My parents came here as immigrants. I was born and raised here myself, and my children were as well. So, I care deeply about the future of Camden and making sure we build a community that we can all be proud of.

Over the years, I’ve seen Camden change hugely, and not for the better. That has to change, as I believe we all deserve more - residents and business owners.

I’m determined to give something back to my community and to make a positive difference in people’s lives. That’s why I’m ready to fight for our future. And if we all work together, I’m certain we can, and will, build a better, cleaner, and safer Camden for us all.

Growing up in a working-class family, I learned early on the importance of family, hard work, and public service. As a Camden council tenant, I’m concerned Camden is failing its residents. It has one of the worst housing repair records of any council in England.
 
Affordable housing must meet local needs without compromising our area’s character. I will support sustainable development that keeps Primrose Hill special while ensuring our infrastructure can accommodate growth.
 
Additionally, I believe small businesses are the heart of Primrose Hill. I want to promote initiatives to protect parking and enhance safety and cleanliness in Primrose Hill village and across our area.

In the end, every step of my journey has been guided by the desire to make a positive impact, helping others where I can.

Since moving to Camden six years 
ago, I have fallen in love with its 
beauty, green spaces, and the diverse 
people who make our community
vibrant. I want to help protect the 
places where we come together, 
through volunteering, music, sport, or 
simply enjoying our urban nature. I’ve 
seen how lack of maintenance and 
development that doesn’t include 
affordable housing or green space can 
erode what makes Camden feel like 
home.

I have dedicated my career in the 
charity sector to protecting and 
restoring the UK’s natural environment. 
My work has involved holding 
government to account and delivering 
practical outcomes for communities 
and nature alike. I hope to bring that 
same approach to Camden, supporting 
the upkeep and enhancement of public 
spaces and helping residents feel 
connected to their neighbourhoods.

I am committed to listening to local 
people and making sure their 
perspectives shape the decisions that 
affect daily life in Camden.

Alexandra has worked as a strategy and transformation consultant for the past 14 years at 3 of the leading professional service firms. She has lead teams across a variety of sectors (energy, pharmaceutics, financial services, transport) helping FTSE100 companies cut costs, become more digital and improve their bottom line.

Alexandra has been working with charities focused on education and children in difficult situations for the past decade. She has been Vice President of Kent UNICEF on campus, supporting their fundraising activities and a school governor at Moss Hall Nursery in West Finchley where she was part of the Fundraising & Staffing and COVID committees. She has worked with charities focused on vulnerable children such as Railway Children, leading projects to support their strategy and operational priorities.

Alexandra has also served as the the National Chair for Conservative Young Women. An experienced campaigner, she was the Deputy Chair Political for the Holborn and St Pancras association and ward chair for the Camden Town and Primrose Hill ward leading campaigning activities through the 2019 General Election, 2021 Mayoral and GLA elections and 2022 local elections. She also stood in the 2022 Local election in the target ward of Primrose Hill.

Alexandra has MBA from INSEAD (where I had a scholarship for extraordinary achievement), an MSc in Political Theory from the London School of Economics (LSE) and a Bachelor in Business Administration (with distinction) from the University of Kent.

I have lived in the South Hampstead ward since 2000 and am very familiar with the area and would be delighted to serve as a councillor.

The areas I would like to see improved upon are the immediate environment, including pollution levels on Finchley Road, as well as the proper disposal of all waste within the ward.

Having served in the Metropolitan Police, I would also like to see a reduction in crime as well as crime prevention initiatives.
Another area I would like to see initiatives in is the care of the elderly, and I would like to see improvement in services and more opportunities, such as a walking programme.

I stand as a Christian, but I will support the Jewish community in standing up against and fighting antisemitism.

I am a hard-working individual and hope you will consider voting for me on the 7th of May 2026.

Andy has long been involved in local politics, with over eight years of experience working across multiple government departments, including in Camden. He is deeply familiar with the political process and has been canvassing since he was 15 years old, when he helped campaign for his father, who stood as a councillor himself. When he was just 16, Andy worked with his local MP, an experience that furthered his interest in Conservative politics.

Andy served as an officer in Camden Council for three years, working on projects that took him across the borough to assist people with a wide range of needs. In this position, he ran the redesign of several governmental services and helped to coordinate emergency COVID-19 relief, leading Camden’s COVID food delivery service to provide meals to hundreds of residents, and organising local vaccination clinics. Andy has also worked in other departments in local government, including Revenues and Benefits, Democratic Services, and Regeneration. Currently, Andy works for a company that runs services on behalf of regional and national governments in the UK and internationally.

Andy is excited about the opportunity to serve the South Hampstead Ward and continue to make a difference in local government, especially in a part of London that has been so significant in his life.

My first steps in local politics have been campaigning against overdevelopment projects that Camden has regrettably wedded itself to. I used my skills in rhetoric, learned from many years as an actor, to challenge the decisions of council planning committees that undermine local conservation areas.

I passionately believe that no one should be left behind in these difficult times with rising costs of living, over-run public services, and scant support for libraries. Our borough needs a revival of scrutiny, not complicity.

You can trust that I will be able to scrutinise the decisions being made at council and cause the necessary disturbances that will lead to a schism. With my determination, I will ensure these cogs turn to make our council run effectively and efficiently, or to prevent further harm to its constituents.

So, if you support me, I will make sure that our council becomes more accountable, more responsive, and ultimately truly representative.

Hello everyone!

My name is Douglas de Morais, and I am standing as a local candidate for South Hampstead on 7th May.

I was a member and supporter of the Conservative Party for over ten years. Over time, however, I became increasingly disillusioned, as the party shifted away from the principles and standards that once defined it. In my view, it has become too focused on internal interests rather than delivering for the country and its taxpayers.

The leadership today no longer reflects the tradition of figures such as Margaret Thatcher or Sir Winston Churchill, individuals many of us respected for their sense of duty, leadership, and commitment to the nation. Instead, there is a growing sense of detachment from the everyday concerns of ordinary people.

Although I was initially sceptical of Reform UK, my experience has changed my perspective. I have met a broad and diverse group of individuals who are united by a shared purpose: to address the real challenges facing our communities. Their focus is not on labels or division, but on practical solutions and restoring pride in our country.

Having lived in Camden for over 15 years, I care deeply about South Hampstead and the wider community. I understand both its strengths and the challenges it faces.

I believe we deserve to feel safe in our streets, confident in our neighbourhoods, and proud of where we live. Too often, that sense of security and pride has been diminished.

It is time for change. It is time to put our communities first again, something I believe the current political establishment is no longer able to deliver.

On 7th May, I ask for your support.

Yours,
Douglas de Morais

The Camden Liberal Democrats are delighted to announce Janet Grauberg as their candidate for the upcoming West Hampstead by-election, now called for Thursday 28th August.

Janet is a long-standing local campaigner who has lived in NW6 for 35 years and has spent years volunteering for our community. From serving as a school governor to co-founding the West Hampstead Community Food Hub during the Covid pandemic, Janet has consistently stepped up to support and advocate for local people.

“I’m offering energy, enthusiasm, and a real care for the community of West Hampstead,” Janet said. “I would be an active, accessible, and strong voice for residents on Camden Council.”

In the 2022 local elections, Janet came just 38 votes short of winning a seat from Labour in West Hampstead—one of the closest results in the borough. Since then, she hasn’t stopped working for local people. Highlights include:

● Leading Lib Dem opposition to the overdevelopment plans for the O2 Centre site. Janet supports building new homes, but believes the plans for 17-storey blocks fail to deliver enough affordable housing or green space for the community.
● Teaming up with Lib Dem Councillor Nancy Jirira (Fortune Green) to campaign against Camden’s messy policy of leaving black bin bags on our high streets.
● Keeping residents informed about key local issues—such as school-run traffic restrictions, planning and licensing applications, and how best to report rough sleeping and anti-social behaviour.

Janet added: “I know I could do so much more if I were elected as your councillor. This by-election is a chance to choose someone who will listen to residents, stand up for this area, and work hard week in, week out, for the local community.

The Liberal Democrats are proud to be standing a candidate with such deep roots in the area and a strong record of community action.

Aarti Wadhwani grew up in Golders Green and attended Channing School in Highgate, before graduating and finance from university.
Aarti has been living in West Hampstead for the past five years and works in Recruitment for an asset management company.
Aarti is a committed community leader, fundraiser and volunteers for Sadhu Vaswani UK, a charity promoting vegetarianism and holds weekly distribution events to feed the homeless in Holborn.
As someone active in our local community, Aarti is keen to support activities for local elderly residents, wants to improve public safety and is very interested in maintaining her local area when it comes to the council achieving value for money, when the council spends our council tax.

Jane Walby has lived in Camden since 1981 and joined the Green Party soon after. Now retired she worked in Special education and later for children’s mental health charities. She has continued her interest in education by being a governor of a local school. Jane has participated in Camden’s public consultations in mental health and adult social care. After living and working in Uganda and Malawi she is passionate about fairtrade and is chair of Camden Fairtrade Network.

Les Levidow has been involved in campaigns for environmental justice and for Palestinian rights since the 1980s. He does academic research with civil society groups on agri-food issues such as alternative supply chains and agroecological methods.

I’m pleased to be standing as a Green Party candidate in the forthcoming Camden local elections in West Hampstead.

I believe we need a politics that matches the scale of the challenges we face, and I’m glad to support the Green Party and its values.

Jason Pritchard is a Camden resident and a Londoner born and bred. He has served as a Councillor in the City of London since 2017 and has been elected on three occasions, representing residents and working on issues affecting local communities.

Jason previously worked in the House of Commons for ten years as a Parliamentary Assistant, supporting parliamentary work on a range of national and local issues.

Alongside his public service, Jason is a proud member of the GMB trade union.

As a councillor he has also served on the Hampstead Heath, Highgate Wood and Queen’s Park Committee, helping oversee and protect some of London’s most important green spaces. He has also been involved in community energy initiatives supporting local renewable power generation. He believes these shared spaces play an important role in the wellbeing of Londoners and the environmental health of the city.

I am standing to represent **the individual**: the working resident, the family, the small business owner, and everyone who builds their life **honestly, rationally, through work and personal responsibility**.

I stand for a rational view of politics that puts **the individual, their freedom, their property and their safety** at the centre.

**Enough of committees that create problems instead of solving them.** Enough of demagogic slogans that dress up waste as **“social justice”, “diversity”, “climate justice” and “multiculturalism”.** Enough of using nice words to justify **bureaucracy, waste and ideological experiments with other people’s money.**

Camden Council must remember one simple truth: **the money it spends is not its money.** It is money taken by force from people who worked for it, created it, paid tax on it, and hoped to build a better life for themselves and their children.

The role of Camden Council is **not** to educate residents, manage their lives, or use them as tools for ideological experiments. Camden should not use residents’ money, council events, grants, housing policy or local services to **attract dependency, promote anti-British values, or support organisations that undermine Britain, the rule of law, individual freedom and Western civilisation.**

Its first, last and only duty is to serve local residents: **the workers, the producers, the value creators.**

**We must strengthen policing. Protect public order. Fight crime and anti-social behaviour without apologies and without hesitation.**

We must restore safety to our streets, stations, businesses and public spaces. **Decent people should not be afraid. Criminals should.**

If elected, I will act as a **Reform representative** to return Camden Council to its true purpose: **protecting the working resident, exposing waste, opposing sectional politics, strengthening local safety, and demanding that every pound of public money serves first the people who created it.**
